item.description:
A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions

by Justin Doty (2021)

Appendix C.2: Labor Dynamics

Figure 17: Impulse Response of Adjustment Shocks to Labor

This plot reports the difference in labor demand between firms hit with low, medium-low, medium-high and high labor shocks and medium labor shocks at different levels labor demand. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low labor shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by medium-low labor shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The third row corresponds to firms hit by medium-high labor shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The last row corresponds to firms hit by high labor shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions

by Justin Doty (2021)

Appendix C.1: Extension of Heterogeneous Estimates from the Main Model

Output Elasticities

Figure 10: Capital Elasticity over Percentiles of Labor and Materials

Figure 11: Labor Elasticity over Percentiles of Capital and Materials

Figure 12: Materials Elasticity over Percentiles of Capital and Labor

Input Efficiencies

Figure 13: Capital Efficiency over Percentiles of Labor and Materials

Figure 14: Labor Efficiency over Percentiles of Capital and Materials

Figure 15: Materials Efficiency over Percentiles of Capital and Labor

Figure 16: Input Marginal Productivities over Percentiles of Capital

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions

by Justin Doty (2021)

Empirical Application with Interactive Plots

Figure 1: Output Elasticities

Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to materials evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of materials averaged over values of capital and labor.

Figure 2: Output Elasticities (over productivity)

Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to materials evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Figure 3: Input Efficiencies

Panel (a) is the estimate of capital efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of labor efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the materials efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of materials averaged over values of capital and labor.

Figure 4: Persistence of Productivity

These are the estimates of average productivity persistence evaluated at percentiles of the innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity.

Figure 5: Input Marginal Productivities

Panel (a) is the estimate of the investment response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of investment demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the labor response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of labor demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the materials response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of investment demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital and labor.

Figure 6: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Productivity

This plot reports the difference in productivity between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of initial productivity. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity.

Figure 7: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Capital

This plot reports the difference in capital between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels investment demand. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand.

Figure 8: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Labor

This plot reports the difference in labor demand between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels labor demand. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand.

Figure 9: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Materials

This plot reports the difference in materials demand between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels materials demand. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions

by Justin Doty (2021)

Appendix C.3: R&D Activities

Figure 18: Productivity Persistence for Non-performing and R&D Performing Firms

Panel (a) is the estimate of the productivity persistence for non-R&D firms evaluated at percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity levels.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the productivity persistence for R&D firms evaluated at percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity levels averaged over R&D.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the productivity persistence for R&D firms evaluated at percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of previous R&D levels averaged over productivity.

Figure 19: Returns to R&D

Panel (a) reports returns to productivity from R&D investment evaluated at percentiles of productivity and percentiles of innovation shocks averaged over R&D. Panel (b) reports similar estimates, evaluated over percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of R&D averaged over productivity.

Figure 20: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Productivity

This plot reports the difference in productivity between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of initial productivity. The solid black line denotes the productivity response of non-R&D firms whereas the dashed green line denotes the response of R&D firms. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity.

Figure 21: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Productivity for Different Levels of R&D

This plot reports the difference in productivity between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of R&D expenditure. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of R&D. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of R&D.

Figure 22: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Capital

This plot reports the difference in capital between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of investment demand. The solid black line denotes the productivity response of non-R&D firms whereas the dashed green line denotes the response of R&D firms. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand.

Figure 23: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Labor

This plot reports the difference in labor between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of labor demand. The solid black line denotes the productivity response of non-R&D firms whereas the dashed green line denotes the response of R&D firms. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand.

Figure 24: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Materials

This plot reports the difference in materials between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of materials demand. The solid black line denotes the productivity response of non-R&D firms whereas the dashed green line denotes the response of R&D firms. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions

by Justin Doty (2021)

Appendix C.4: Correcting for Selection Bias

Figure 25: Output Elasticities

Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to materials evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of materials averaged over values of capital and labor.

Figure 26: Output Elasticities (over productivity)

Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to materials evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of productivity averaged over values of capital, labor, and materials.

Figure 27: Input Efficiencies

Panel (a) is the estimate of capital efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of labor efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the materials efficiency evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of materials averaged over values of capital and labor.

Figure 28: Persistence of Productivity

These are the estimates of average productivity persistence evaluated at percentiles of the innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity.

Figure 29: Input Marginal Productivities

Panel (a) is the estimate of the investment response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of investment demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital.

Panel (b) is the estimate of the labor response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of labor demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital.

Panel (c) is the estimate of the materials response to productivity evaluated at percentiles of investment demand and percentiles of productivity averaged over capital and labor.

Figure 30: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Productivity

This plot reports the difference in productivity between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of initial productivity. The solid black line denotes the selection-corrected productivity response whereas the dashed blue dashed line denotes the response conditional on staying. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of initial productivity.

Figure 31: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Capital

This plot reports the difference in capital between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of investment demand. The solid black line denotes the selection-corrected productivity response whereas the dashed blue dashed line denotes the response conditional on staying. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of investment demand.

Figure 32: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Labor

This plot reports the difference in labor between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of labor demand. The solid black line denotes the selection-corrected productivity response whereas the dashed blue dashed line denotes the response conditional on staying. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of labor demand.

Figure 33: Impulse Response of an Innovation Shock to Materials

This plot reports the difference in materials between firms hit with low and high productivity shocks and medium productivity shock at different levels of materials demand. The solid black line denotes the selection-corrected productivity response whereas the dashed blue dashed line denotes the response conditional on staying. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand. The second row corresponds to firms hit by high productivity shocks at year 2. From left to right, these correspond to firms with low, medium, and high levels of materials demand.
